title: Chapter 7 Quiz
description: Test your mastery of Data Lists!

questions.question: What is an Array?
options: A function that returns multiple values.
A single variable name that can hold a list of multiple values of the same data type.
questions.explanation: An array lets you group together many identical variables (like 100 ints for 100 scores) under one shared name.

questions.question: How do you correctly declare an array of 10 integers in C?
questions.explanation: C always uses square brackets `[]` after the variable name to declare array sizes.

questions.question: In C, the very first element in an array is located at Index 1.
questions.explanation: Arrays in C are 'Zero-Indexed'. The first element is always at Index 0.

questions.question: If you have an array `float prices[4];`, what are the valid index numbers you can use to safely access data?
questions.explanation: Because counting starts at 0, an array of size 4 has accessible slots at exactly 0, 1, 2, and 3.

questions.question: What happens in C if you attempt to assign a value to an index that is larger than your array size (Out of Bounds)?
options: The compiler safely crashes with an 'Out of Bounds' error.
C expands the array automatically to fit the new data.
C blindly writes the data to that raw memory address, potentially corrupting other parts of your program.
questions.explanation: C does not hold your hand. It will do exactly what you tell it to do, even if it means writing over critical memory data belonging to other variables, which leads to catastrophic bugs.

questions.question: Arrays and ______ are a perfect match because the tracker variable `i` can be used to cycle through every index instantly.
questions.explanation: A `for` loop is the standard and perfect way to iterate completely over every slot in an array.

questions.question: How does C actually create a 'String' of text like "Dog" under the hood?
options: It uses a special 64-bit 'string' primitive data type.
It creates an array of individual `char` letters: ['D', 'o', 'g'].
It stores them as negative integers.
It relies on the operating system to handle text.
questions.explanation: C does not have a real String type! It simply groups characters together sequentially in memory using a basic array.

questions.question: What is the 'Null Terminator' `\0`?
options: A bug that stops the program.
An invisible marker placed at the very end of character arrays to tell C where the string stops.
A command to clear the screen.
questions.explanation: Because strings are just raw arrays of characters, C uses `\0` as a stop-sign so functions like `printf` know when to stop reading.

questions.question: A string defined as `char name[] = "Cat";` consumes exactly 3 bytes of memory.
questions.explanation: It consumes 4 bytes! 'C' (1 byte), 'a' (1 byte), 't' (1 byte), and the invisible Null Terminator '\0' (1 byte).

questions.question: What happens if a string accidentally loses its Null Terminator `\0`?
options: Functions will keep reading memory past the end of the string until they hit a random 0 byte or crash.
Nothing, C knows how long it is anyway.
questions.explanation: Without the stop-sign, C blindly continues reading adjacent RAM as if it were part of the string, printing garbage text.

questions.question: Are you allowed to copy one string into another by typing `string1 = string2;`?
options: No, because you cannot assign one array directly to another in C. You must use `strcpy`.
Yes, but only if they are the exact same length.
questions.explanation: Because strings are just array memory addresses, `=` doesn't copy the text. You must loop over each character carefully using a library function.

questions.question: To use essential string manipulation functions like `strcpy` and `strlen`, you must include `<______>` at the top of your file.
questions.explanation: The `string.h` library contains the tools to make working with character arrays bearable.

questions.question: What does the `strlen()` function do?
options: It measures the total size of the array in RAM.
It counts how many visible characters are in the string before hitting the Null Terminator.
It changes the letters to uppercase.
questions.explanation: `strlen` literally loops through the array, counting 1 for each letter, and stopping when it sees `\0`.

questions.question: If you want to compare if two strings are exactly the same word, how do you do it?
questions.explanation: You cannot use `==` because it will just compare the memory addresses! You must use `strcmp` and check if the alphabetically calculated difference is `0`.

questions.question: In this array: `int scores[] = {10, 20, 30};`, what value does `scores[2]` hold?
questions.explanation: Index 0 is 10. Index 1 is 20. Index 2 is 30.

questions.question: Arrays can hold different data types in the same array (e.g., an int and a float together).
questions.explanation: Arrays in C are strictly typed. An `int array[]` can ONLY hold integers.

questions.question: What does `strcpy(destination, source);` do?
options: Copies the word from 'destination' over to 'source'.
Copies the word from 'source' to 'destination', overwriting what was there.
questions.explanation: It reads the text from the `source` argument and copies it character-by-character into the memory of the `destination` argument.

questions.question: What happens if you initialize an array like this: `int arr[5] = {1, 2};`?
options: Syntax Error. You must provide exactly 5 numbers.
The remaining 3 slots are filled with random memory garbage.
The remaining 3 slots are automatically initialized to Zero (0).
questions.explanation: When you partially initialize an array in C, it helpfully fills the rest of the available slots with 0s.

questions.question: Why do programmers frequently use `size_t` when dealing with `strlen()`?
options: Because `strlen` returns a strictly positive size type `size_t`, which represents the maximum size of objects on that system.
It makes the string print faster.
questions.explanation: `size_t` is the technically correct, entirely positive integer type for representing sizes and lengths of things in memory.

questions.question: You create `char password[5];` and use `strcpy` to copy "SuperSecretPassword" into it. What happens?
options: C automatically expands `password` to fit the long string.
C safely stops copying after 5 characters.
A "Buffer Overflow" occurs. C blindly writes the extra 14 characters over top of adjacent memory, potentially causing a catastrophic crash.
questions.explanation: This is a Buffer Overflow, one of the most famous and dangerous security vulnerabilities in C. `strcpy` does not check boundaries!
